example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) : Fin.snoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
  exact Fin.snoc_castSucc a f i

-- 3. tail_cons
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : Fin.tail (Fin.cons a f) = f := by
  exact Fin.tail_cons

-- 4. funext
example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) : f = g := by
  funext i; exact h i

-- 5. Building tuples via lambda + match (the standard approach)

  exact ⟨fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩

-- 6. Proving properties of cons-built tuples
-- Given a cons-built tuple, prove what happens at position 0
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : a = 42) :
    Fin.cons a f (0 : Fin 3) = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_zero]
  exact h

    (fun i : Fin 2 => f i) = f := by
  funext i
  rfl

-- 8. The "define by cases then use" pattern
-- Define a specific function, prove it has the right values
example : ∃ f : Fin 2 → ℕ, f 0 = 5 ∧ f 1 = 10 := by
  exact ⟨fun | 0 => 5 | 1 => 10, rfl, rfl⟩

-- 9. Cons + tail round trip
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : Fin.tail (Fin.cons a f) = f := by
  exact Fin.tail_cons

-- 10. Cons preserves head
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : (Fin.cons a f) 0 = a := by
  exact Fin.cons_zero a f

-- 11. Snoc preserves body

example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) :
    Fin.snoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by

    Fin.cons a f = (Fin.cons a g : Fin 3 → ℕ) := by

  exact Fin.snoc_last a f

  exact Fin.snoc_castSucc a f i

-- P5: exact Fin.tail_cons
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : Fin.tail (Fin.cons a f) = f := by
  exact Fin.tail_cons

-- P6: funext

  funext i; exact h i

    (h : Fin.cons a f (0 : Fin 3) = 42) : a = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_zero] at h
  exact h

    Fin.cons a f = (Fin.cons b g : Fin 3 → ℕ) := by
  subst ha
  have : f = g := funext hfg
  rw [this]

-- P9: Build tuple via fun notation, then prove cons relationship

  funext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact (Fin.cons_zero 1 _).symm
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero =>
      show 2 = Fin.cons 1 (fun i : Fin 2 => i.val + 2) ⟨1, by omega⟩
      rw [show (⟨1, by omega⟩ : Fin 3) = (⟨0, by omega⟩ : Fin 2).succ from rfl]
      exact (Fin.cons_succ 1 _ ⟨0, by omega⟩).symm
    | succ m =>

        show 3 = Fin.cons 1 (fun i : Fin 2 => i.val + 2) ⟨2, by omega⟩
        rw [show (⟨2, by omega⟩ : Fin 3) = (⟨1, by omega⟩ : Fin 2).succ from rfl]

    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

    (h : Fin.cons a f i.succ = 42) : f i = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_succ] at h
  exact h

    (h : Fin.snoc f a (Fin.last 2) = 42) : a = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.snoc_last] at h
  exact h

-- P12: init_snoc (analog of tail_cons for snoc)
-- init : (∀ i, α i) → (i : Fin n) → α i.castSucc
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ) : Fin.init (Fin.snoc f a) = f := by
  exact Fin.init_snoc

-- P13: Concrete evaluation with rfl
-- When everything is concrete, rfl works
example : Fin.cons (10 : ℕ) (Fin.cons 20 (fun i : Fin 0 => Fin.elim0 i)) (0 : Fin 2) = 10 := by
  rfl

example : Fin.cons (10 : ℕ) (Fin.cons 20 (fun i : Fin 0 => Fin.elim0 i)) (1 : Fin 2) = 20 := by
  rfl

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Tuple.Basic

-- VERIFIED PATTERNS (all compile):

    Fin.cons a f (0 : Fin 3) = a := by

    Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i := by
  exact Fin.cons_succ a f i

-- snoc_last
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ) :
    Fin.snoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a := by
  exact Fin.snoc_last a f

-- snoc_castSucc
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) :
    Fin.snoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
  exact Fin.snoc_castSucc a f i

-- tail_cons
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    Fin.tail (Fin.cons a f) = f := by
  exact Fin.tail_cons

-- funext
example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
    f = g := by
  funext i
  exact h i

-- rw at hypotheses
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ)
    (h : Fin.cons a f (0 : Fin 3) = 42) : a = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_zero] at h
  exact h

example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (i : Fin 2)
    (h : Fin.cons a f i.succ = 42) : f i = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_succ] at h
  exact h

example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ)
    (h : Fin.snoc f a (Fin.last 2) = 42) : a = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.snoc_last] at h
  exact h

-- funext with case split
example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = g 0) (h1 : f 1 = g 1) (h2 : f 2 = g 2) :
    f = g := by
  funext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- cons/tail round trip in a multi-step proof
example (a b : ℕ) (f g : Fin 2 → ℕ) (ha : a = b) (hfg : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
    Fin.cons a f = (Fin.cons b g : Fin 3 → ℕ) := by
  subst ha
  have : f = g := funext hfg
  rw [this]
ENDLEAN

file.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Tuple.Basic

-- VERIFIED PATTERNS (all compile):

-- cons_zero
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    Fin.cons a f (0 : Fin 3) = a := by
  exact Fin.cons_zero a f

-- cons_succ
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (i : Fin 2) :
    Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i := by
  exact Fin.cons_succ a f i

-- snoc_last
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ) :
    Fin.snoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a := by
  exact Fin.snoc_last a f

-- snoc_castSucc
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) :
    Fin.snoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
  exact Fin.snoc_castSucc a f i

-- tail_cons
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    Fin.tail (Fin.cons a f) = f := by
  exact Fin.tail_cons

-- funext
example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
    f = g := by
  funext i
  exact h i

-- rw at hypotheses
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ)
    (h : Fin.cons a f (0 : Fin 3) = 42) : a = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_zero] at h
  exact h

example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (i : Fin 2)
    (h : Fin.cons a f i.succ = 42) : f i = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_succ] at h
  exact h

example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (a : ℕ)
    (h : Fin.snoc f a (Fin.last 2) = 42) : a = 42 := by
  rw [Fin.snoc_last] at h
  exact h

-- funext with case split
example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = g 0) (h1 : f 1 = g 1) (h2 : f 2 = g 2) :
    f = g := by
  funext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- cons/tail round trip in a multi-step proof
example (a b : ℕ) (f g : Fin 2 → ℕ) (ha : a = b) (hfg : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
    Fin.cons a f = (Fin.cons b g : Fin 3 → ℕ) := by
  subst ha
  have : f = g := funext hfg
  rw [this]

The `Fin.cons` is dependent, so `Fin.cons a f` where `a : ℕ` and `f : Fin 2 → ℕ` — Lean needs to figure out that `α = fun _ => ℕ`, but it can't because `f : Fin 2 → ℕ` doesn't directly unify with `(i : Fin 2) → (fun _ => ℕ) i.succ`.
 
The types should be definitionally equal since `(fun _ => ℕ) i.succ` reduces to `ℕ`, making `(i : Fin 2) → (fun _ => ℕ) i.succ` the same as `Fin 2 → ℕ`. But the elaborator might not be reducing that lambda application before attempting unification, which is a known limitation when inferring types in dependent type systems.

import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Tuple.Basic

example : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 20 := by rfl
example : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 2 = 30 := by rfl

example (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = b := by rfl
example (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 2 = c := by rfl

-- 3. Cons = vec notation (by rfl)
example (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons a ![b, c] := by rfl

-- 4. Tail computation (by rfl)
example (a b c : ℕ) : Fin.tail (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![b, c] := by rfl

-- 5. Matrix.cons_val_zero and Matrix.cons_val_succ
#check @Matrix.cons_val_zero  -- vecCons x u 0 = x
#check @Matrix.cons_val_succ  -- does this exist?

    f = ![10, 20, 30] := by
  funext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

example (a b c : ℕ) :
    Fin.tail (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = c := by rfl

example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) : f = g ↔ (f 0 = g 0 ∧ f 1 = g 1 ∧ f 2 = g 2) := by
  constructor

cat > /home/ahart/Documents/autogame/lean-4-game-creator/finite_math/Game/TestTuple.lean << 'ENDLEAN'
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Tuple.Basic

-- === ADDITIONAL PATTERNS ===

-- Matrix.cons_val_zero in a proof
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    Matrix.vecCons a f 0 = a := by
  exact Matrix.cons_val_zero a f

-- Matrix.cons_val_succ in a proof
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (i : Fin 2) :
    Matrix.vecCons a f i.succ = f i := by
  exact Matrix.cons_val_succ a f i

-- ![a, b, c] is Matrix.vecCons
-- So Matrix.cons_val_zero works for ![a, b, c] 0 = a
example (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a := by
  exact Matrix.cons_val_zero a ![b, c]

-- And Matrix.cons_val_succ for accessing later elements
example (a b c : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) i.succ = (![b, c] : Fin 2 → ℕ) i := by
  exact Matrix.cons_val_succ a ![b, c] i

-- Boss pattern: build a tuple and prove it has the right structure
-- Given that we know individual values, reconstruct the tuple
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 4) (h2 : f 2 = 9) :
    f = ![1, 4, 9] := by
  funext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Boss pattern: prove a property of a specific tuple
example : ∀ i : Fin 3, (![2, 4, 6] : Fin 3 → ℕ) i % 2 = 0 := by
  intro ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Tail decomposition: f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)
-- This should be true by definition
-- Check: does Fin.cons_self_tail exist?
#check @Fin.cons_self_tail

-- Test head + tail reconstruction
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) := by
  exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm

-- This is great for the world: the key identity is
-- f = cons (f 0) (tail f)
-- i.e., every tuple is the head prepended to the tail

-- Test: empty tuple uniqueness
example (f : Fin 0 → ℕ) : f = Fin.elim0 := by
  funext ⟨_, h⟩
  exact absurd h (by omega)

-- Actually, simpler:
example (f : Fin 0 → ℕ) : f = Fin.elim0 := by
  ext ⟨_, h⟩
  exact absurd h (by omega)

-- Test: single element tuple
example (f : Fin 1 → ℕ) : f = ![f 0] := by
  funext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n => exact absurd hv (by omega)
ENDLEAN

Title "Finite Mathematics"
Introduction
"
# Finite Mathematics

Finite sets, finsets, multisets, lists, and finite sums/products.

This course covers the finite-object infrastructure: `Fin`, `Fintype`, `Finset`,
finite sets as subtypes, lists versus multisets, permutations of lists, finitely
supported functions, big operators, matrices over finite index types, binomial
coefficients, factorials, and counting identities.

This course is under construction.
"

file.content:
#### W03: FinTuples (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand that `Fin n -> α` is a "tuple" (ordered list of fixed length), and will be able to construct and decompose tuples using `Fin.cons` and `Fin.snoc`.

**Theorem families**:
- `Fin.cons` — prepend an element to a tuple
- `Fin.snoc` — append an element to a tuple
- `Fin.tail` — drop the first element
- `Fin.cons_zero`, `Fin.cons_succ`
- `Fin.snoc_last`, `Fin.snoc_castSucc`
- Function extensionality for `Fin n -> α`

**Proof-move goals**:
- Build a function `Fin n -> α` by specifying each value
- Use `Fin.cons` to build tuples recursively
- Decompose a tuple using `Fin.tail`
- Prove two tuples are equal by proving they agree at every index (function extensionality)

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition Fin.cons Fin.snoc Fin.tail`
- `NewTheorem Fin.cons_zero Fin.cons_succ Fin.snoc_last Fin.snoc_castSucc`
- `NewTactic funext` — function extensionality (new tactic)

**Level sketch**:
- L01: `Fin n -> α` IS a tuple. Define a function `Fin 3 -> ℕ` by cases. Lesson: functions from Fin as data.
- L02: `Fin.cons` — build a tuple by prepending. Lesson: recursive tuple construction.
- L03: `Fin.cons_zero` and `Fin.cons_succ` — access elements of a `cons`-built tuple. Lesson: destructor lemmas.
- L04: `Fin.snoc` — build a tuple by appending. Lesson: alternative construction.
- L05: `Fin.tail` — drop the first element. Lesson: tuple decomposition.
- L06: Function extensionality (`funext`). Prove two tuples are equal by proving pointwise equality. Lesson: ext for functions.
- L07 (Boss): Build a tuple, decompose it, and prove an equality. Requires `cons`, access lemmas, and `funext`.

**Dependencies**: FinNavigation

**Pset partner**: PsetFin (W04)

---

#### W04: PsetFin (Problem Set)

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me redesign the world based on what actually compiles. Key findings from testing:

cat > /home/ahart/Documents/autogame/lean-4-game-creator/finite_math/Game/TestTuple.lean << 'ENDLEAN'
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

-- L01: Functions as Data
-- Statement: Evaluate a function from Fin
example : (fun (i : Fin 3) => 2 * i.val + 1) 1 = 3 := by rfl

-- L02: Vec Notation
-- Statement: Access an element of a vec-notation tuple
example : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 20 := by rfl

-- L03: Variable Tuples
-- Statement: Vec notation works with variables
example (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a := by rfl

-- L04: Cons Structure
-- Statement: ![a,b,c] is the same as Fin.cons a ![b,c]
example (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons a ![b, c] := by rfl

-- L05: Dropping the Head (Fin.tail)
example (a b c : ℕ) : Fin.tail (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![b, c] := by rfl

-- L06: Head-Tail Reconstruction (Fin.cons_self_tail)
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) : Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f := by
  exact Fin.cons_self_tail f

-- L07: ext for Functions (function extensionality)
-- The learner already knows ext from MeetFin (for Fin elements).
-- Now show it works for functions too.
example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) : f = g := by
  ext i
  exact h i

-- L08: Proving Tuple Equality (coached)
-- Given individual values, prove f equals a specific tuple
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 4) (h2 : f 2 = 9) :
    f = ![1, 4, 9] := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- L09: Boss Part 1 — reconstruction identity
-- Every tuple equals cons (head) (tail)
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) := by
  exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm

-- L09: Boss Part 2 — prove tuple identity from pointwise data
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 2) (h2 : f 2 = 3) :
    f = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- L09: Boss combined statement
example :
    (∀ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)) ∧
    (∀ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 1 → f 1 = 2 → f 2 = 3 → f = ![1, 2, 3]) := by
  constructor
  · intro f
    exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
  · intro f h0 h1 h2
    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
    cases v with
    | zero => exact h0
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero => exact h1
      | succ m =>
        cases m with
        | zero => exact h2
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Additional test: does Matrix.cons_val_zero work?
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : Matrix.vecCons a f 0 = a := by
  exact Matrix.cons_val_zero a f

-- Test: building a function by match then comparing to vec
example : (fun (i : Fin 3) => i.val ^ 2) = ![0, 1, 4] := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Test: empty tuple
example (f g : Fin 0 → ℕ) : f = g := by
  ext ⟨_, h⟩
  exact absurd h (by omega)
ENDLEAN

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 1

Title "Functions as Data"

Introduction "
# Functions from Fin as Tuples

In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:

**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.

Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.

This identification — *functions from a finite index set = tuples* —
is pervasive in mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^n$ is a function
$\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\to \\mathbb{R}$. A sequence of length $n$ is a
function from `Fin n`.

**Your task**: Build a specific 3-tuple — a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`
whose values at 0, 1, 2 are 10, 20, 30.

The goal is `∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, ...`. Use `use` to provide the
function, then prove it has the right values.

To define a function by listing its values on each input, write:
`fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
"

/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
  Hint "Use `use` to provide the function. Try:
  `use fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`"
  use fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30
  Hint "The function has been defined. Now prove the three equalities.
  Since each value is determined by the definition, `exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`
  closes all three goals at once."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`"
  exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩

Conclusion "
You just built a 3-tuple by defining a function from `Fin 3`.

The key idea: writing `fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
is exactly like writing the tuple $(10, 20, 30)$. Each clause
says what the function returns at that index.

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 1

Title "Functions as Data"

Introduction "
# Functions from Fin as Tuples

In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:

**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.

Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.

This identification — *functions from a finite index set = tuples* —
is pervasive in mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^n$ is a function
$\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\to \\mathbb{R}$. A sequence of length $n$ is a
function from `Fin n`.

**Your task**: Build a specific 3-tuple — a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`
whose values at 0, 1, 2 are 10, 20, 30.

The goal is `∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, ...`. Use `use` to provide the
function, then prove it has the right values.

To define a function by listing its values on each input, write:
`fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
"

/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
  Hint "Use `use` to provide the function. Try:
  `use fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`"
  use fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30
  Hint "The function has been defined. Now prove the three equalities.
  Since each value is determined by the definition, `exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`
  closes all three goals at once."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`"
  exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩

Conclusion "
You just built a 3-tuple by defining a function from `Fin 3`.

The key idea: writing `fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
is exactly like writing the tuple $(10, 20, 30)$. Each clause
says what the function returns at that index.

After `use`, the goal asked you to verify each component.
Since the values are determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
each equation.

In the next level, you'll learn a cleaner notation for writing
these tuples.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 2

Title "Vec Notation"

Introduction "
# The `![...]` Notation

Writing `fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30` works, but it's
verbose. Lean provides a much cleaner notation:

$$\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]}$$

This creates the same function `Fin 3 → ℕ`. The `!` prefix
distinguishes this from a plain list — it's a **vector** (a
function from `Fin n`), not a linked list.

Element access is straightforward: if `v = ![10, 20, 30]`,
then `v 0 = 10`, `v 1 = 20`, `v 2 = 30`. These equalities
hold by definition — `rfl` closes them.

**Your task**: Verify that the second element of `![10, 20, 30]`
is `20`. (Remember: indexing starts at 0, so the *second*
element is at index 1.)
"

/-- The element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]` is 20. -/
Statement : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 20 := by
  Hint "The value `![10, 20, 30] 1` reduces to `20` by definition.
  Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
`![10, 20, 30]` is syntactic sugar for a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`.
Accessing any element is a computation that Lean can do
automatically, so `rfl` suffices.

You can build tuples of any length:
- `![5] : Fin 1 → ℕ` — a 1-tuple
- `![3, 7] : Fin 2 → ℕ` — a pair
- `![1, 2, 3, 4] : Fin 4 → ℕ` — a 4-tuple

And they work with any type, not just `ℕ`:
- `![true, false] : Fin 2 → Bool`
- `![\"hello\", \"world\"] : Fin 2 → String`

The type annotation (`: Fin n → α`) is sometimes needed to
help Lean figure out the types.

In the next level, you'll see that this notation also works
with variables, not just concrete numbers.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 3

Title "Variable Tuples"

Introduction "
# Tuples with Variables

The `![...]` notation doesn't just work with concrete numbers.
You can use variables too:

If `a b c : ℕ`, then `![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ` is a 3-tuple
whose values are `a`, `b`, and `c`.

Element access still reduces by definition:
- `![a, b, c] 0 = a`
- `![a, b, c] 1 = b`
- `![a, b, c] 2 = c`

These hold by `rfl` because `![a, b, c]` is defined to return
`a` at index 0, `b` at index 1, and `c` at index 2 — regardless
of what `a`, `b`, `c` actually are.

**Your task**: Prove that the first element of `![a, b, c]` is `a`.
"

/-- Accessing the first element of a variable tuple. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a := by
  Hint "Even with variables, element access is definitional. Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
The pattern: `![a, b, c] i` reduces to the `i`-th element by
definition. This works because `![...]` is internally defined
using `Fin.cons`, which routes each index to the corresponding
element.

This is analogous to subscript notation in mathematics:
if $v = (a, b, c)$, then $v_0 = a$, $v_1 = b$, $v_2 = c$.
In Lean, `v 0`, `v 1`, `v 2` play the role of subscripts.

In the next level, you'll see the underlying machinery:
`Fin.cons`, which builds tuples by prepending one element
at a time.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 4

Title "Prepending with Fin.cons"

Introduction "
# Fin.cons: Building Tuples One Element at a Time

Under the hood, `![a, b, c]` is built using **`Fin.cons`** —
a function that prepends one element to an existing tuple.

`Fin.cons` takes:
- An element `a : α` (the new head)
- A tuple `f : Fin n → α` (the existing tail)

and produces a tuple `Fin.cons a f : Fin (n + 1) → α` that
starts with `a` followed by the elements of `f`.

The notation `![a, b, c]` is shorthand for:
$$\\texttt{Fin.cons } a \\;(\\texttt{Fin.cons } b \\;(\\texttt{Fin.cons } c \\;\\texttt{!![]}))$$
where `!![]` is the empty tuple.

More concretely:
$$\\texttt{![a, b, c]} = \\texttt{Fin.cons } a \\;\\texttt{![b, c]}$$

This is the tuple analog of how `List.cons` builds lists —
prepend one element at a time.

**Your task**: Prove that `![a, b, c]` equals `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`.
This is true by definition.
"

/-- `![a, b, c]` is the same as `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
    (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons a ![b, c] := by
  Hint "This equality holds by definition — `![a, b, c]`
  IS `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons` is the fundamental building block for tuples:
- Start with the empty tuple `!![] : Fin 0 → α`
- Prepend `c`: `Fin.cons c !![] = ![c] : Fin 1 → α`
- Prepend `b`: `Fin.cons b ![c] = ![b, c] : Fin 2 → α`
- Prepend `a`: `Fin.cons a ![b, c] = ![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → α`

Each `Fin.cons` increases the tuple length by 1.

This is the `Fin` analog of how $\\texttt{cons}$ builds linked lists.
The difference: a linked list has variable length, while
`Fin n → α` has the length `n` baked into the type.

In the next level, you'll learn to decompose tuples: if
`Fin.cons` builds by prepending, `Fin.tail` decomposes by
dropping the head.
"

/-- `Fin.cons a f` prepends element `a` to tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.cons a f      -- explicit
![a, b, c]        -- ![a, b, c] = Fin.cons a ![b, c]
```

## Type
`Fin.cons : α → (Fin n → α) → (Fin (n + 1) → α)`

## Element access
- `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` (the new head)
- `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` (the old tail)

## Example
`Fin.cons 10 ![20, 30] = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.cons as "Fin.cons"

NewDefinition Fin.cons

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 5

Title "Dropping the Head"

Introduction "
# Fin.tail: Decomposing Tuples

If `Fin.cons` builds tuples by prepending, **`Fin.tail`**
decomposes them by dropping the first element.

Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.tail f` is the
tuple `Fin n → α` that skips the first element:

$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i + 1)$$

For example:
$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]} = \\texttt{![20, 30]}$$

The first element `10` is dropped, and the remaining elements
`20, 30` form a shorter tuple.

**Your task**: Prove that dropping the head of `![a, b, c]`
gives `![b, c]`.
"

/-- `Fin.tail` drops the first element of a tuple. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
    Fin.tail (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![b, c] := by
  Hint "`Fin.tail` of `![a, b, c]` is `![b, c]` by definition.
  Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons` and `Fin.tail` are inverses in a sense:

- `Fin.cons` prepends: `Fin.cons a ![b, c] = ![a, b, c]`
- `Fin.tail` drops: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`

Together, they give you the head and the tail of any tuple.
The head is `f 0` (just evaluate at index 0), and the tail is
`Fin.tail f`.

This decomposition — *every non-empty tuple is a head followed
by a tail* — is the tuple analog of how every non-empty list
is a head `::` tail. You'll use this pattern heavily when
reasoning about tuples by induction on their length.

In the next level, you'll see the formal identity that ties
these pieces together.
"

/-- `Fin.tail f` drops the first element of tuple `f`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.tail f    -- drop the head
```

## Type
`Fin.tail : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`

## Definition
`Fin.tail f i = f i.succ`

## Example
`Fin.tail ![10, 20, 30] = ![20, 30]`

## Key identity
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` — every tuple equals
its head prepended to its tail.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.tail as "Fin.tail"

NewDefinition Fin.tail

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 6

Title "Head-Tail Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Head + Tail

You've seen that `Fin.cons` prepends and `Fin.tail` drops.
The fundamental identity connecting them is:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f$$

In words: if you take the head of `f` (which is `f 0`) and
prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you
get back `f` itself.

This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
and its tail.

In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.

**Your task**: Prove this identity. The theorem
`Fin.cons_self_tail f` states `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
Note the direction — you need `f = ...`, so you'll use `.symm`.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) := by
  Hint "The theorem `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states:
  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
  Your goal has the equality reversed, so use `.symm`:
  `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`"
  exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons_self_tail` is the key structural fact about tuples.
It says:

> Decomposing a tuple into head + tail and reassembling gives
> back the original tuple.

This will be essential later when you reason about tuples by
induction on length. The base case handles the empty tuple
(`Fin 0 → α`), and the inductive step decomposes
`Fin (n + 1) → α` into a head and a `Fin n → α` tail.

In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two tuples are
equal by checking them index-by-index — using `ext`.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states that
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail.

## Syntax
```
exact Fin.cons_self_tail f         -- proves cons (f 0) (tail f) = f
exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm  -- proves f = cons (f 0) (tail f)
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its first element
and the remaining elements.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cons_self_tail as "Fin.cons_self_tail" in "Fin"

NewTheorem Fin.cons_self_tail

prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you

This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
and its tail.

In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.

**Your task**: Prove this identity. The theorem
`Fin.cons_self_tail f` states `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
Note the direction — you need `f = ...`, so you'll use `.symm`.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) := by
  Hint "The theorem `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states:
  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
  Your goal has the equality reversed, so use `.symm`:
  `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`"
  exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons_self_tail` is the key structural fact about tuples.
It says:

> Decomposing a tuple into head + tail and reassembling gives
> back the original tuple.

This will be essential later when you reason about tuples by
induction on length. The base case handles the empty tuple
(`Fin 0 → α`), and the inductive step decomposes
`Fin (n + 1) → α` into a head and a `Fin n → α` tail.

In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two tuples are
equal by checking them index-by-index — using `ext`.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states that
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail.

## Syntax
```
exact Fin.cons_self_tail f         -- proves cons (f 0) (tail f) = f
exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm  -- proves f = cons (f 0) (tail f)
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its first element
and the remaining elements.
-/

NewTheorem Fin.cons_self_tail

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 7

Title "ext for Functions"

Introduction "
# Extensionality for Functions

In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
equal by showing their values are equal. The `ext` tactic is
more general than that — it works for *any* type that has an
extensionality principle.

For **functions**, extensionality says:

> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.

In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.

When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n → α`, using
`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.

This is function extensionality — the principle that functions
are determined by their values. It's a fundamental axiom in
Lean's type theory.

**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
"

/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
    f = g := by
  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
  ext i
  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
  `∀ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
  exact h i

Conclusion "
The pattern is simple:

1. `ext i` — reduce function equality to pointwise equality
2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`

In MeetFin, `ext` reduced `Fin` equality to value equality.
Here, `ext` reduces function equality to pointwise equality.
Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
are equal when their components are equal.

Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
for functions. Both are valid.

In the next level, you'll combine `ext` with case analysis to
prove a function equals a specific tuple.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 8

Title "Proving Tuple Equality"

Introduction "
# Identifying a Tuple from Its Values

Suppose you know the value of `f` at every index:
`f 0 = 1`, `f 1 = 4`, `f 2 = 9`. Can you conclude that
`f = ![1, 4, 9]`?

Yes — by function extensionality. The strategy:

1. `ext` to reduce `f = ![1, 4, 9]` to `f i = ![1, 4, 9] i`
2. Case-split on `i` to handle each index separately
3. At each concrete index, use the corresponding hypothesis
   and the fact that `![1, 4, 9]` reduces by definition

The case split is the same nested `cases` pattern you used
in MeetFin for `Fin 3`. After destructuring `i` as `⟨v, hv⟩`,
split on `v`: the cases `0`, `1`, `2` correspond to the three
hypotheses, and the impossible case `v ≥ 3` is closed by
`absurd hv (by omega)`.

**Your task**: Given the values of `f` at each index, prove
`f = ![1, 4, 9]`.
"

/-- A function is determined by its values at each index. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ)
    (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 4) (h2 : f 2 = 9) :
    f = ![1, 4, 9] := by
  Hint "Start with `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to introduce the index and
  reduce to `f ⟨v, hv⟩ = ![1, 4, 9] ⟨v, hv⟩`."
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  Hint "Now case-split on `v` with `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`.
  The `zero` case corresponds to index 0."
  cases v with
  | zero =>
    Hint "The goal is `f 0 = ![1, 4, 9] 0`, which simplifies to `f 0 = 1`.
    You have `{h0} : f 0 = 1`. Use `exact h0`."
    exact h0
  | succ n =>
    Hint (hidden := true) "Continue splitting: `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
    cases n with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `f 1 = 4`. Use `exact h1`."
      exact h1
    | succ m =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Split once more on `m`."
      cases m with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `f 2 = 9`. Use `exact h2`."
        exact h2
      | succ k =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
        Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
        exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
The `ext` + case split pattern is the standard way to prove
a function from `Fin n` equals a specific tuple:

```
ext ⟨v, hv⟩
cases v with
| zero => ...      -- handle index 0
| succ n =>
  cases n with
  | zero => ...    -- handle index 1
  | succ m => ...  -- handle index 2, etc.
```

The impossible branch (where `v ≥ n`) is always closed by
`exact absurd hv (by omega)`.

This pattern scales to any `Fin n`, though it gets verbose
for large `n`. (Later, you'll learn tactics like `fin_cases`
that automate this case split.)

In the boss level, you'll combine all the tuple moves:
construction, decomposition, and equality proofs.
"

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 9

Title "Computing with Tuples"

Introduction "
# Recognizing a Computed Tuple

You now know how to verify that a function equals a specific
tuple when given its values as hypotheses. What about when
the function is defined by a formula?

Consider `fun (i : Fin 3) => i.val ^ 2`. This computes:
- At `i = 0`: $0^2 = 0$
- At `i = 1`: $1^2 = 1$
- At `i = 2`: $2^2 = 4$

So this function should equal `![0, 1, 4]`.

The proof strategy is the same: `ext` to reduce to pointwise
equality, then case-split. At each concrete index, both sides
compute to the same number, so `rfl` closes the goal.

**Your task**: Prove that squaring gives `![0, 1, 4]`.
"

/-- The squaring function on Fin 3, expressed as a tuple. -/
Statement :
    (fun (i : Fin 3) => i.val ^ 2) = ![0, 1, 4] := by
  Hint "Use `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to introduce the index, then case-split on `v`."
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => rfl | succ n => ...`"
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases n with | zero => rfl | succ m => ...`"
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m`, then `rfl` or `absurd`."
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
When both sides of the equality are computable, each case
reduces to a concrete equation that `rfl` handles.

The general pattern for showing `fun i => expr i = ![a, b, c]`:
1. `ext` to introduce the index
2. Case-split to reach a concrete index
3. `rfl` at each concrete case (both sides evaluate to the same number)
4. `absurd` for the impossible case

This is the same `ext` + case split pattern from the previous
level, just with `rfl` replacing the hypotheses.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 10

Title "Boss: Tuple Fundamentals"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting Tuple Tools Together

Time to combine all the tuple moves you've learned.

**Part 1 — Head-Tail Reconstruction**: Every tuple `f` equals
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`. This uses `Fin.cons_self_tail`
(Level 6).

**Part 2 — Identifying from Values**: Given the values of `f`
at each index, prove `f = ![1, 2, 3]`. This uses `ext` + case
split (Levels 7–8).

Strategy for Part 1:
- `intro f`
- `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`

Strategy for Part 2:
- `intro f h0 h1 h2`
- `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` then case-split
- Use `h0`, `h1`, `h2` for each case; `absurd` for impossible
"

/-- The head-tail reconstruction identity, and tuple identification. -/
Statement :
    (∀ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)) ∧
    (∀ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 1 → f 1 = 2 → f 2 = 3 → f = ![1, 2, 3]) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: head-tail reconstruction
  · Hint "Part 1: prove every tuple equals cons (head) (tail).
    Start with `intro f`."
    intro f
    Hint "Use `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`."
    exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
  -- Part 2: identifying from values
  · Hint "Part 2: identify `f` from its values at each index.
    Start with `intro f h0 h1 h2`."
    intro f h0 h1 h2
    Hint "Use `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to introduce the index."
    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
    Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`. For each valid case
    (0, 1, 2), use the corresponding hypothesis. For the
    impossible case (v >= 3), use `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h0`"
      exact h0
    | succ n =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Continue splitting on `n`."
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h1`"
        exact h1
      | succ m =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m`."
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h2`"
          exact h2
        | succ k =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "`exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
          exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
Congratulations — you've completed **Fin Tuples**!

Here's your tuple toolkit:

| Tool | What it does | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| `fun \\| 0 => a \\| 1 => b \\| ...` | Build tuple by cases | Level 1 |
| `![a, b, c]` | Build tuple with vec notation | Level 2 |
| `![a, b, c] i` = the `i`-th element | Access by `rfl` | Level 3 |
| `Fin.cons a f` | Prepend element to tuple | Level 4 |
| `Fin.tail f` | Drop the first element | Level 5 |
| `Fin.cons_self_tail f` | `f = cons (f 0) (tail f)` | Level 6 |
| `ext i` | Reduce function equality to pointwise | Level 7 |
| `ext` + case split | Prove `f = ![...]` by checking each index | Level 8 |

**The big picture**: `Fin n → α` models ordered tuples. The
notation `![a, b, c]` makes them concrete. The `cons`/`tail`
decomposition lets you reason about tuples inductively.
Function extensionality (`ext`) lets you prove two tuples
are equal by checking them index by index.

**Where this is going**: Now that you can build and manipulate
tuples, you're ready for the Pset (problem set) world, which
will test these skills on fresh problems without scaffolding.
After that, you'll meet `Finset` — *unordered* finite
collections — and see how ordered tuples (functions from Fin)
connect to unordered sets.
"

import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L01_FunctionsAsData
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L02_VecNotation
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L03_VariableTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L04_PrependingWithCons

import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L06_HeadTailReconstruction
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L07_ExtForFunctions
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L08_ProvingTupleEquality
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L09_ComputingWithTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L10_Boss

World "FinTuples"
Title "Fin Tuples"

Introduction "
# Fin Tuples: Functions as Ordered Data

You've learned that `Fin n` is the finite index set
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$, and you can navigate within it using
`last`, `castSucc`, and `succ`. Now comes the payoff:

**A function `Fin n → α` is an `n`-tuple.**

This is one of the most useful identifications in formalized
mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^3$ is a function
`Fin 3 → ℝ`. A sequence of length 5 is a function
`Fin 5 → α`. The entries of an $n \\times m$ matrix are a
function `Fin n → Fin m → ℝ`.

The advantage of using functions instead of lists: the length
is part of the type. You can't accidentally add a 3-vector to
a 4-vector — the types don't match.

In this world, you'll learn to:
- **Build** tuples using `![a, b, c]` notation and `Fin.cons`
- **Access** elements by evaluation (index the function)
- **Decompose** tuples into head + tail using `Fin.tail`
- **Prove** two tuples are equal using function extensionality (`ext`)

The central tool is **`ext`**: two tuples are equal if and only
if they agree at every index. You already know `ext` for `Fin`
elements (two `Fin` values are equal when their natural numbers
are equal). For functions, `ext` says two functions are equal
when they agree on all inputs.

The boss at the end will ask you to combine construction,
decomposition, and extensionality in a single proof.
"

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 1

Title "Functions as Data"

Introduction "
# Functions from Fin as Tuples

In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:

**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.

Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.

This identification — *functions from a finite index set = tuples* —
is pervasive in mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^n$ is a function
$\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\to \\mathbb{R}$. A sequence of length $n$ is a
function from `Fin n`.

**Your task**: Build a specific 3-tuple — a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`
whose values at 0, 1, 2 are 10, 20, 30.

The goal is `∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, ...`. Use `use` to provide the
function, then prove it has the right values.

To define a function by listing its values on each input, write:
`fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
"

/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
  Hint "Use `use` to provide the function. Try:
  `use fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`"
  use fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30
  Hint "The function has been defined. Now prove the three equalities.
  Since each value is determined by the definition, `exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`
  closes all three goals at once."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`"
  exact ⟨rfl, rfl, rfl⟩

Conclusion "
You just built a 3-tuple by defining a function from `Fin 3`.

The key idea: writing `fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
is exactly like writing the tuple $(10, 20, 30)$. Each clause
says what the function returns at that index.

After `use`, the goal asked you to verify each component.
Since the values are determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
each equation.

In the next level, you'll learn a cleaner notation for writing
these tuples.
"

`symm` is not yet introduced in any world. But `(Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm` uses `.symm` as a method on the proof term, not as a tactic. The warning says "No world introducing symm" — the GameServer tracks which tactics/terms are used and warns if they haven't been formally introduced.

I have two options:
1. Add `NewTactic symm` or `NewHiddenTactic symm` to introduce it
2. Change the proof to avoid `.symm`

Option 2: I could rewrite the statement so the equality goes in the natural direction: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`. Then `exact Fin.cons_self_tail f` without `.symm`.


The pedagogical trade-off is that `f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)` puts the unknown on the left, which feels more natural, but flipping it to `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` still works fine and avoids the `.symm` entirely. I'm leaning toward just changing the statement direction to sidestep this issue.

file.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 6

Title "Head-Tail Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Head + Tail

You've seen that `Fin.cons` prepends and `Fin.tail` drops.
The fundamental identity connecting them is:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f$$

In words: if you take the head of `f` (which is `f 0`) and
prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you
get back `f` itself.

This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
and its tail.

In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.

**Your task**: Prove this identity. The theorem
`Fin.cons_self_tail f` states `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
Note the direction — you need `f = ...`, so you'll use `.symm`.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) := by
  Hint "The theorem `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states:
  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
  Your goal has the equality reversed, so use `.symm`:
  `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`"
  exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons_self_tail` is the key structural fact about tuples.
It says:

> Decomposing a tuple into head + tail and reassembling gives
> back the original tuple.

This will be essential later when you reason about tuples by
induction on length. The base case handles the empty tuple
(`Fin 0 → α`), and the inductive step decomposes
`Fin (n + 1) → α` into a head and a `Fin n → α` tail.

In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two tuples are
equal by checking them index-by-index — using `ext`.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states that
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail.

## Syntax
```
exact Fin.cons_self_tail f         -- proves cons (f 0) (tail f) = f
exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm  -- proves f = cons (f 0) (tail f)
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its first element
and the remaining elements.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cons_self_tail as "Fin.cons_self_tail" in "Fin"

NewTheorem Fin.cons_self_tail

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num

tool_use_result.originalFile:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 6

Title "Head-Tail Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Head + Tail

You've seen that `Fin.cons` prepends and `Fin.tail` drops.
The fundamental identity connecting them is:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f$$

In words: if you take the head of `f` (which is `f 0`) and
prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you
get back `f` itself.

This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
and its tail.

In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.

**Your task**: Prove this identity. The theorem
`Fin.cons_self_tail f` states `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
Note the direction — you need `f = ...`, so you'll use `.symm`.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) := by
  Hint "The theorem `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states:
  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
  Your goal has the equality reversed, so use `.symm`:
  `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`"
  exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons_self_tail` is the key structural fact about tuples.
It says:

> Decomposing a tuple into head + tail and reassembling gives
> back the original tuple.

This will be essential later when you reason about tuples by
induction on length. The base case handles the empty tuple
(`Fin 0 → α`), and the inductive step decomposes
`Fin (n + 1) → α` into a head and a `Fin n → α` tail.

In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two tuples are
equal by checking them index-by-index — using `ext`.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states that
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail.

## Syntax
```
exact Fin.cons_self_tail f         -- proves cons (f 0) (tail f) = f
exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm  -- proves f = cons (f 0) (tail f)
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its first element
and the remaining elements.
-/

tool_use_result.originalFile:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 10

Title "Boss: Tuple Fundamentals"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting Tuple Tools Together

Time to combine all the tuple moves you've learned.

**Part 1 — Head-Tail Reconstruction**: Every tuple `f` equals
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`. This uses `Fin.cons_self_tail`
(Level 6).

**Part 2 — Identifying from Values**: Given the values of `f`
at each index, prove `f = ![1, 2, 3]`. This uses `ext` + case
split (Levels 7–8).

Strategy for Part 1:
- `intro f`
- `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`

Strategy for Part 2:
- `intro f h0 h1 h2`
- `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` then case-split
- Use `h0`, `h1`, `h2` for each case; `absurd` for impossible
"

/-- The head-tail reconstruction identity, and tuple identification. -/
Statement :
    (∀ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)) ∧
    (∀ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 1 → f 1 = 2 → f 2 = 3 → f = ![1, 2, 3]) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: head-tail reconstruction
  · Hint "Part 1: prove every tuple equals cons (head) (tail).
    Start with `intro f`."
    intro f
    Hint "Use `exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`."
    exact (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
  -- Part 2: identifying from values
  · Hint "Part 2: identify `f` from its values at each index.
    Start with `intro f h0 h1 h2`."
    intro f h0 h1 h2
    Hint "Use `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to introduce the index."
    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
    Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`. For each valid case
    (0, 1, 2), use the corresponding hypothesis. For the
    impossible case (v >= 3), use `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h0`"
      exact h0
    | succ n =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Continue splitting on `n`."
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h1`"
        exact h1
      | succ m =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m`."
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "`exact h2`"
          exact h2
        | succ k =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "`exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
          exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
Congratulations — you've completed **Fin Tuples**!

Here's your tuple toolkit:

| Tool | What it does | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| `fun \\| 0 => a \\| 1 => b \\| ...` | Build tuple by cases | Level 1 |
| `![a, b, c]` | Build tuple with vec notation | Level 2 |
| `![a, b, c] i` = the `i`-th element | Access by `rfl` | Level 3 |
| `Fin.cons a f` | Prepend element to tuple | Level 4 |
| `Fin.tail f` | Drop the first element | Level 5 |
| `Fin.cons_self_tail f` | `f = cons (f 0) (tail f)` | Level 6 |
| `ext i` | Reduce function equality to pointwise | Level 7 |
| `ext` + case split | Prove `f = ![...]` by checking each index | Level 8 |

**The big picture**: `Fin n → α` models ordered tuples. The
notation `![a, b, c]` makes them concrete. The `cons`/`tail`
decomposition lets you reason about tuples inductively.
Function extensionality (`ext`) lets you prove two tuples
are equal by checking them index by index.

**Where this is going**: Now that you can build and manipulate
tuples, you're ready for the Pset (problem set) world, which
will test these skills on fresh problems without scaffolding.
After that, you'll meet `Finset` — *unordered* finite
collections — and see how ordered tuples (functions from Fin)
connect to unordered sets.
"

content.content:
1→import Game.Metadata
     2→import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation
     3→
     4→World "FinTuples"
     5→Level 1
     6→
     7→Title "Functions as Data"
     8→
     9→Introduction "
    10→# Functions from Fin as Tuples
    11→
    12→In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
    13→natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:
    14→
    15→**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
    16→ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.
    17→
    18→Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
    19→what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
    20→order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
    21→defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.
    22→
    23→This identification — *functions from a finite index set = tuples* —
    24→is pervasive in mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^n$ is a function
    25→$\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\to \\mathbb{R}$. A sequence of length $n$ is a
    26→function from `Fin n`.
    27→
    28→**Your task**: Build a specific 3-tuple — a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`
    29→whose values at 0, 1, 2 are 10, 20, 30.
    30→
    31→The goal is `∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, ...`. Use `use` to provide the
    32→function, then prove it has the right values.
    33→
    34→To define a function by listing its values on each input, write:
    35→`fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
    36→"
    37→
    38→/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
    39→Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
    40→  Hint "Use `exact` to provide the function and proof at once. Try:
    41→  `exact ⟨fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`
    42→

file.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 1

Title "Functions as Data"

Introduction "
# Functions from Fin as Tuples

In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:

**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.

Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.

This identification — *functions from a finite index set = tuples* —
is pervasive in mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^n$ is a function
$\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\to \\mathbb{R}$. A sequence of length $n$ is a
function from `Fin n`.

**Your task**: Build a specific 3-tuple — a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`
whose values at 0, 1, 2 are 10, 20, 30.

The goal is `∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, ...`. Use `use` to provide the
function, then prove it has the right values.

To define a function by listing its values on each input, write:
`fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
"

/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
  Hint "Use `exact` to provide the function and proof at once. Try:
  `exact ⟨fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`

  Here `fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30` defines the function by
  listing its values at each index. The three `rfl`s verify the values."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`"
  exact ⟨fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩

Conclusion "
You just built a 3-tuple by defining a function from `Fin 3`.

The key idea: writing `fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30`
is exactly like writing the tuple $(10, 20, 30)$. Each clause
says what the function returns at that index.

After `use`, the goal asked you to verify each component.
Since the values are determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
each equation.

In the next level, you'll learn a cleaner notation for writing
these tuples.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num

content.thinking:
L01 is a one-liner proof (`exact ⟨...⟩`). This is not very interactive. The learner types one line and the level is done. Let me consider whether to change the statement to something more interactive, or accept this as a simple introductory level.

Looking at MeetFin L01: `Statement : Fin 5 := by exact ⟨3, by omega⟩` — also a one-liner. And FinNavigation L01: `Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by exact Fin.val_last 4` — also a one-liner.


So one-liner levels are acceptable for early introductory content where the conceptual work happens in the intro and the proof is just the interactive exercise. The hints need better layering though — the visible hint should explain the strategy while the hidden hint provides the full answer. Since there's only one line, that's really all the structure I can manage here.

Now I'm checking whether the proof actually works. Both `exact` with the anonymous constructor and `use` with the function definition should work, since `use` automatically closes the remaining goals from the conjunction. I'll go with `use` to keep it consistent with what the intro mentions.

import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L01_FunctionsAsData
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L02_VecNotation
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L03_VariableTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L04_PrependingWithCons
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L05_DroppingTheHead
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L06_HeadTailReconstruction
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L07_ExtForFunctions
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L08_ProvingTupleEquality
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L09_ComputingWithTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L10_Boss

World "FinTuples"
Title "Fin Tuples"

Introduction "
# Fin Tuples: Functions as Ordered Data

You've learned that `Fin n` is the finite index set
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$, and you can navigate within it using
`last`, `castSucc`, and `succ`. Now comes the payoff:

**A function `Fin n → α` is an `n`-tuple.**

This is one of the most useful identifications in formalized
mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^3$ is a function
`Fin 3 → ℝ`. A sequence of length 5 is a function
`Fin 5 → α`. The entries of an $n \\times m$ matrix are a
function `Fin n → Fin m → ℝ`.

The advantage of using functions instead of lists: the length
is part of the type. You can't accidentally add a 3-vector to
a 4-vector — the types don't match.

In this world, you'll learn to:
- **Build** tuples using `![a, b, c]` notation and `Fin.cons`
- **Access** elements by evaluation (index the function)
- **Decompose** tuples into head + tail using `Fin.tail`
- **Prove** two tuples are equal using function extensionality (`ext`)

The central tool is **`ext`**: two tuples are equal if and only
if they agree at every index. You already know `ext` for `Fin`
elements (two `Fin` values are equal when their natural numbers
are equal). For functions, `ext` says two functions are equal
when they agree on all inputs.

The boss at the end will ask you to combine construction,
decomposition, and extensionality in a single proof.
"

content.text:

| Level | Title | Dominant Lesson | New Burden | Proof Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|---|
